summary: In my home, we often talked about the importance of Black Lives Matter and the fight against racism. This organization came up especially when discussing events like the wrong done to Philando Castile and other racial injustices. Although we didn't discuss our own racial identities much, Black Lives Matter was a key part of understanding the broader societal issues around race. It helped frame conversations about equality and justice in America during my upbringing.

We talked about the wrong done to Philando Castile in my family discussions about race and justice. His case was one of the examples that highlighted the ongoing racial issues in America that we needed to understand. Although we didn't focus on our own racial identities, Philando Castile's story was a significant part of the conversations about racism and the need for change. It made me aware of the real consequences of racial injustice in the country.

At home, my father spoke to us in Tagalog, which was a constant part of our daily life. Despite this, no one ever labeled us as mixed or biracial, even though Tagalog was a clear marker of our heritage. Speaking Tagalog connected me to my mother's family and our Filipino roots, even though I was surrounded mostly by white people in school and work. It was a language that tied me to my culture, even if my racial identity was complicated.

I worked at Caribou Coffee in Eagan, Minnesota, where most of my coworkers spoke Spanish. This environment often reminded me of my multiracial identity, as people would speak Spanish to me without knowing if I understood it. My job at Caribou Coffee placed me in a mostly white social circle, which influenced how I saw myself racially. It was a place where I experienced subtle reminders of my identity and cultural differences.

I lived and worked in Eagan, Minnesota, where my social environment was predominantly white. This setting shaped much of my early understanding of race and identity, as I was surrounded by white family, friends, and coworkers. Eagan was the backdrop for many of my experiences that made me question and eventually embrace my biracial identity. It was also where I worked at Caribou Coffee, which further influenced my racial awareness.

The death of Michael Brown was one of the key events that marked the beginning of what I call the war on race in America. At that time, I publicly defended the officers and courts involved, denying that racism was still an issue. This event, along with the Christmas protests at the Mall of America, shaped my early views on race and justice. It was also a period when my brother and I both identified as White, choosing to side with dominant groups.

The Christmas protests at the Mall of America were part of the racially charged events that influenced my early views on race. During this time, I defended the authorities and denied the existence of racism in America. The protests at such a prominent place highlighted the tensions around race and justice in the country. These events coincided with my brother and me identifying as White and taking the side of dominant groups.
